topic: SAA western cell west of 45°W — post-EXP-225 integration review
change_summary: EXP-225 integrated 2026-04-22. detail_evidence 1143 → 2293 chars (new 200-word opening paragraph prepended engaging 45°W threshold, >100-year falsifiability horizon, axial-symmetry structural argument, and Terra-Nova/Pavón-Carrasco citations). detail_verdict_text 1079 → 1173 chars (post-hoc boilerplate localized to WMM-2025 / CHAOS-7 and 45°W threshold). c3 H1 (duplicate sentence) and c3 H2 (TLDR range) confirmed fixed in prior cycles. c3 H3, H4, H5 now addressed by this patch.
our_argument_summary: Post-integration, WIN-040 now opens with a WIN-040-specific paragraph that (a) engages the 45°W threshold claim with the cell centre location (~80°W), drift rate (0.2–0.3°/yr) and >100-year falsifiability horizon, and (b) deploys the axial-symmetry structural argument (H(r) depends only on r, zero azimuthal derivative, cannot derive any longitude-specific SAA feature). Grouped data-reuse meta-argument preserved unchanged after the new opening. detail_verdict_text boilerplate localized to WMM-2025 / CHAOS-7 and the 45°W threshold. Verdict remains Std Model Explains.

relevant_files: raw-text/03-predictions.txt (W022: "SAA western cell position: West of 45 deg W, cells separated (PENDING)")
dome_code_analysis.calculation_pipeline: No automated monitoring for W022. Predicate is evaluated editorially against WMM/CHAOS-7 outputs. No hardcoded check and no live fetch for longitude thresholds.
dome_code_analysis.hardcoded_vs_computed: Neither. Structural threshold evaluated manually against external geomagnetic model outputs.
dome_code_analysis.escape_clauses: Threshold 45°W is ~35° east of current western-cell centre (~80°W in the review prose, though see H2 below — likely imprecise). Cell drifts westward; reversing direction and crossing the threshold requires >>100 years of unprecedented reversal. "Cells separated" has no minimum separation requirement.
dome_code_analysis.code_discrepancies: None new. H(r) formula is correctly quoted from raw-text (8537·exp(-r/8619) km). Axial symmetry of H(r) is geometrically self-evident.

code_analysis_tags.monitoring_evidence: No automated domain for SAA cell longitude in monitor.py snapshots from any cycle. W022 is a manual editorial check against WMM/CHAOS-7.
code_analysis_tags.relabels_standard_evidence: SAA cell longitudes are direct outputs of WMM-2025 / CHAOS-7 / IGRF-13 derived from geodynamo MHD of reversed-flux patches at the core-mantle boundary. The dome relabels the driving mechanism as aetheric field decay but adopts the same numerical position.
code_analysis_tags.post_hoc_evidence: The SAA western cell has been observed west of 45°W since the early 20th century. W022 registered 2026-03-12, many decades after the observation was well-established and publicly available in WMM/CHAOS-7 outputs.
code_analysis_tags.derives_from_dome_evidence: H(r) = 8537·exp(-r/8619) km depends only on radial distance r from the Axis Mundi. It has zero azimuthal derivative — no dome equation published on the site has any longitude dependence. The 45°W numeric threshold is adopted from WMM/CHAOS, not derived from dome geometry.

holes_found.description: FABRICATED CITATION (primary): the new paragraph cites "Terra-Nova et al. 2017, PNAS 114:3543–3548" hyperlinked to DOI 10.1073/pnas.1615340114. This DOI does not exist in Crossref (returns 404 on api.crossref.org/works/10.1073/pnas.1615340114), and a Crossref search for Terra-Nova papers in PNAS returns zero results. The Crossref search for "South Atlantic Anomaly" papers in PNAS across all of 2017 returns zero hits. There IS a legitimate Terra-Nova et al. 2017 SAA paper — "Relating the South Atlantic Anomaly and geomagnetic flux patches," Physics of the Earth and Planetary Interiors 266:39–53, DOI 10.1016/j.pepi.2017.03.002 — but it is in PEPI, not PNAS, and the DOI, volume, and page range in our text are all wrong. A reader who clicks the citation hyperlink gets a dead link. This is exactly the kind of error a dome defender could screenshot as evidence that we fabricate citations while accusing them of sloppy methodology. Critical severity: a single bad DOI in a reviewed piece of prose is a credibility-level defect.
holes_found.recommendation: Replace the citation with the real paper: Terra-Nova & Amit 2017, Physics of the Earth and Planetary Interiors 266:39–53, DOI 10.1016/j.pepi.2017.03.002. Hyperlink to https://doi.org/10.1016/j.pepi.2017.03.002 (verified resolvable via Crossref). The substantive claim (SAA positions derive from core-mantle-boundary reversed-flux patches) is supported by this real paper so only the citation metadata needs to change. Do this as a surgical patch of detail_evidence — replace the exact substring "Terra-Nova et al. 2017, PNAS 114:3543–3548" with "Terra-Nova & Amit 2017, Phys. Earth Planet. Inter. 266:39–53" and the href with "https://doi.org/10.1016/j.pepi.2017.03.002".

holes_found.description: FABRICATED CITATION (secondary): the same paragraph cites "Pávón-Carrasco & De Santis 2016, Earth-Science Reviews 154:96–125" for SAA historical drift rates. This citation is also wrong. The real Pavón-Carrasco & De Santis 2016 paper on SAA drift is "The South Atlantic Anomaly: The Key for a Possible Geomagnetic Reversal," Frontiers in Earth Science 4:40, DOI 10.3389/feart.2016.00040 (verified via Crossref). A Crossref search for Pavón-Carrasco & De Santis papers in Earth-Science Reviews returns zero hits; Earth-Science Reviews volume 154 (2016) is a real volume but does not contain a paper by these authors on this topic. The same credibility concern applies — we have a second fabricated citation in the same paragraph, which compounds the problem: if one bad citation can be argued as a transcription error, two in one paragraph reads as systematic fabrication.
holes_found.recommendation: Replace with the correct citation: Pavón-Carrasco & De Santis 2016, Frontiers in Earth Science 4:40, DOI 10.3389/feart.2016.00040. Since this citation currently has no hyperlink (plain text), just the metadata substring needs changing. Recommended substring replacement: "Pávón-Carrasco & De Santis 2016, Earth-Science Reviews 154:96–125" → "Pavón-Carrasco & De Santis 2016, Frontiers in Earth Science 4:40" (and drop the diacritical á on Pavón as the author typically writes it without — minor). These two citation fixes MUST ship together in a single patch; leaving either one in place is a credibility liability.

holes_found.description: H(r) mislabeled as "field equation". The new paragraph calls H(r) = 8537·exp(-r/8619) "the dome's field equation," but H(r) is the firmament-HEIGHT function, not a geomagnetic field equation. The dome's raw text consistently describes H(r) as a height/geometry function (raw-text/01-home.txt: "H(r) Firmament Curve: 8537 x e^(-r/8619) km"). The dome does use H(r)/r as a derived "Field Strength Factor" (FSF) for geomagnetic predictions (raw-text/03-predictions.txt:22: "FSF = field strength factor from V12 H(r)/r dome geometry"; raw-text/08-live-power.txt:149: "Polaris Excess ↔ Latitude: r=0.997 ("H(r)/r geometric prediction")"). A dome defender could correctly respond: "You confuse H(r) (firmament height) with our field equation. Our field strength depends on H(r)/r, not H(r) directly." Our axial-symmetry argument still holds (H(r)/r is also radially symmetric), but the label is wrong. Not critical because the axial-symmetry conclusion is correct, but the defender opening is real.
holes_found.recommendation: Tighten the label. Either (a) use "the dome's primary geometric equation H(r) = 8537·exp(-r/8619)" (acknowledges it is geometric rather than field-directly), or (b) add H(r)/r explicitly: "the dome's field-strength factor H(r)/r, derived from H(r) = 8537·exp(-r/8619), depends only on radial distance r from the Axis Mundi and has zero azimuthal derivative." Option (b) is preferred because it directly uses the dome's own FSF terminology and pre-empts the semantic defender objection. The conclusion sentence ("longitude-specific SAA features cannot be derived from dome geometry at all") is unchanged because FSF inherits H(r)'s radial symmetry.

holes_found.description: Cell centre "near 80°W" is an imprecise and likely too-western value. The American-cell SAA centroid in recent WMM/CHAOS-7 outputs (2020–2025) is more commonly placed around 60–70°W, not 80°W. At 0.2–0.3°/yr westward drift, 2016 (when Pavón-Carrasco wrote) cell-centre values of ~60°W would by 2025 be at ~62–65°W, not 80°W. If the actual current cell centre is at ~65°W, then the 45°W threshold sits ~20° east of centre (not ~35°), and the >100-year horizon at 0.25°/yr reversed drift drops to ~80 years. Still a very long horizon and the argument still holds, but the specific numbers overclaim distance. A dome defender would be correct to point out "your 80°W cell centre is off by 10–15°." The ~80°W value may come from a single-decade snapshot or a different altitude/threshold convention; without a specific cited source inside the paragraph for the 80°W value (WMM 2025 is gestured at but no specific cell-centre coordinates are pinned), the number is load-bearing but un-sourced.
holes_found.recommendation: Replace "a centre near 80°W" with a more defensible phrasing: "a centre near 65–75°W (exact value depends on altitude and threshold convention; see Finlay et al. 2020 for CHAOS-7 SV analysis)" OR simply "a centre in the mid-Atlantic well west of 45°W." Re-compute the threshold-to-centre gap with the corrected value and adjust "~35° east of current cell centre" accordingly (likely ~20–25°). Also update the matching phrasing in the c3 dome_code_analysis.escape_clauses field which also says ~80°W — that text likely was auto-propagated. The >100-year horizon argument is robust to cell-centre precision; the precise number is not.

holes_found.description: Queue entry 229's reason text attributes the integration to "EXP-226" — but EXP-226 is the WIN-053 third-horn-precision expansion; the actual WIN-040 patch is EXP-225. The queue-reason typo is minor (no content impact), but it surfaced because queue-entry reasons are authored by the decider and not independently verified. Flagged only to catch a systematic issue: queue reasons can reference the wrong EXP without any checker catching it. For this review no action required beyond noting the misattribution.
holes_found.recommendation: No content change. Recommend that tinker or integrity add a cross-check: each queue entry's reason text should name an EXP that either (a) exists in monitor/analyst/expansions/ AND is a direct source of the patch, or (b) is the correct EXP-ID for the target_id. This would be a low-cost structural-integrity pattern.

holes_found.description: Diacritical inconsistency: the author's name is rendered as "Pávón-Carrasco" (á with acute) but the canonical spelling across Crossref and the author's own papers is "Pavón-Carrasco" (unaccented Pa-, accented ó only). Not load-bearing but reads as a minor transcription artifact.
holes_found.recommendation: Fix to "Pavón-Carrasco" when applying the citation correction above. A single-character replace.

holes_found.description: The TLDR factually correct but misses the new structural headline. tldr_evidence/tldr_verdict continue to describe WIN-040 through the grouped-batch data-reuse frame. The analyst intentionally kept the TLDRs unchanged (documented in EXP-225 tldr_change field). This is a defensible choice — the structural-impossibility argument lives in the expanded detail and would bloat a TLDR — but a reader who never expands misses the single strongest point. Not a factual error, so not a major, but there is a framing trade-off.
holes_found.recommendation: No change required. Flagged for transparency. A possible future micro-edit: append one clause to tldr_verdict (e.g., "; the dome has no equation with longitude dependence") if space allows without wreck to TLDR concision.

stronger_arguments.description: The two fabricated citations could be turned from critical liabilities into strengths by replacing them with real sources that say exactly what we want. Terra-Nova & Amit 2017 (PEPI 266:39–53) is directly on-topic for SAA-from-reversed-flux-patches — no need to find another paper. Pavón-Carrasco & De Santis 2016 (Frontiers in Earth Science 4:40) is even more prominent as the canonical SAA-as-reversal-precursor reference. After replacement, the new paragraph's citation strength INCREASES because both corrected references are well-known, open-access, and carry stronger name-recognition than the fabricated ones did.
sources: Terra-Nova & Amit 2017, Phys. Earth Planet. Inter. 266:39–53, DOI 10.1016/j.pepi.2017.03.002 (verified via Crossref)
Pavón-Carrasco & De Santis 2016, Frontiers in Earth Science 4:40, DOI 10.3389/feart.2016.00040 (verified via Crossref)

stronger_arguments.description: If cell centre is corrected to ~65°W and threshold-to-centre gap becomes ~20°, the argument can be sharpened by adding a second quantification: even at the fastest observed historical SAA drift reversal episodes (paleo-magnetic, ~2°/decade in extreme episodes; see Amit et al. 2021 EPS 73:38), crossing 20° would still take >100 years. This swaps a single-number horizon for a bounded horizon with an absolute-worst-case backstop and makes the "unfalsifiable in practice" claim more robust.
sources: Amit, Terra-Nova, Lézin, Trindade 2021, Earth, Planets and Space 73:38, DOI 10.1186/s40623-021-01356-w (verified via Crossref — non-monotonic SAA growth and motion)

kernel_of_truth.description: The analyst's structural strategy is sound: lead with a WIN-specific engagement, deploy the axial-symmetry argument as the strongest physics-level point, preempt the "you also post-hoc" defender move by reframing the discriminator as "derivation path vs. end-state adoption." This is exactly the c3 recommended fix, competently executed at the prose level. The grouped meta-argument is preserved unchanged, which preserves batch-level coherence.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: Strategic competence at the prose level doesn't save the patch when two of its three new citations are fabricated. The axial-symmetry argument stands on its own (geometric fact about radial symmetry, independent of external references), but the two bogus citations undermine it by association: a dome defender scanning for holes will find the fabricated DOI first and use it to discredit the whole paragraph. Fixing the citations is a one-line surgical patch — there is no reason not to ship it immediately.

advocate_mode.best_defense: A dome defender's strongest move post-integration is: "You fabricated two citations in a single paragraph while accusing us of sloppy methodology. The Terra-Nova 2017 PNAS paper doesn't exist. The Pavón-Carrasco & De Santis 2016 Earth-Science Reviews paper doesn't exist. Your own OpenTimestamps critique (Section 1.4) depends on the claim that we handle citations carelessly; your own review demonstrates the same failure mode. If your citations are unreliable, why should I trust your H(r) analysis?" This scores a full 5/5 because it is factually correct — until the citations are fixed. After the fix, the defense collapses back to the ~2/5 range the axial-symmetry argument naturally puts it at.
advocate_mode.preemptive_recommendation: Fix both citations in a same-day surgical patch before any dome reader scans the page. The patches are trivial: two string replacements in detail_evidence. Queue an ISS-NNNN (critical severity, citation_error) and assign directly to the decider for immediate self-apply (no EXP required — surgical substring fix). This is the fastest remediation: the defender's advantage exists only while the fabricated DOIs remain live; a 15-minute patch turns a 5/5 defense into a 2/5 one.

cross_win_consistency.inconsistencies_found: WIN-053 argues the dome's radially-symmetric B(r) cannot produce localized SAA anomaly structure. WIN-040's new opening paragraph argues the dome's radially-symmetric H(r) cannot produce longitude-specific SAA features. Both are correct but use DIFFERENT equation names (B(r) vs H(r)) for essentially the same argument. A defender could argue "which is it — B(r) or H(r)?" Actually both are radially symmetric in the dome model, so the argument holds for both, but the WIN-040/WIN-053 pair should pick consistent terminology. Recommend cross-WIN pass after the citation fix to unify "field equation" / "FSF" / "H(r)/r" terminology across the two WINs.

claims_checked: Pavón-Carrasco & De Santis 2016 Earth-Science Reviews 154:96–125 — Crossref author+journal search
Crossref search for SAA papers in PNAS 2017
Terra-Nova paper list via Crossref (author+SAA query)
H(r) formula 8537·exp(-r/8619) km against raw-text/ sources
Cell centre "near 80°W" claim against common WMM/CHAOS-7 values (flagged as likely imprecise, not verified against a specific published 2025 value)
quantitative_verification.computations: Crossref query results: (a) DOI 10.1073/pnas.1615340114 → 404; (b) Crossref search for Terra-Nova papers in PNAS container → 0 results; (c) Crossref search for SAA papers in PNAS 2017 → 0 results; (d) the real Terra-Nova 2017 SAA paper is PEPI 266:39–53 (DOI 10.1016/j.pepi.2017.03.002, verified resolvable); (e) Pavón-Carrasco & De Santis 2016 SAA paper is Frontiers in Earth Science 4:40 (DOI 10.3389/feart.2016.00040, verified resolvable); (f) Pavón-Carrasco & De Santis have no paper in Earth-Science Reviews per Crossref; (g) H(r) formula matches raw-text/01-home.txt, 04-coordinates.txt, 07-ai-context.txt exactly.
quantitative_verification.discrepancies: Two fabricated citations (CRITICAL). H(r) label as "field equation" is imprecise (MAJOR). Cell centre "near 80°W" is likely too-western by ~10–15° (MODERATE). All captured above in holes_found.

deeper_analysis:
Cycle 4 review of WIN-040 after EXP-225 integration (priority-queue item 229, pushed 2026-04-22T18:29:52.257Z). The patch set was strategically correct — the c3 review recommended exactly this structure (WIN-specific opening, axial-symmetry argument, localized post-hoc boilerplate), and the analyst executed it competently at the prose level. However the patch introduced TWO FABRICATED CITATIONS that together amount to a critical-severity defect.

Citation 1: Terra-Nova et al. 2017, PNAS 114:3543–3548, DOI 10.1073/pnas.1615340114. Crossref DOI lookup: 404. Crossref search for Terra-Nova in PNAS: 0 hits. Crossref search for SAA papers in PNAS 2017: 0 hits. The real Terra-Nova 2017 SAA paper is "Relating the South Atlantic Anomaly and geomagnetic flux patches," Phys. Earth Planet. Inter. 266:39–53, DOI 10.1016/j.pepi.2017.03.002 (Terra-Nova & Amit). Same topic, same year, entirely different journal and DOI. The hyperlink in detail_evidence resolves to nothing.

Citation 2: Pavón-Carrasco & De Santis 2016, Earth-Science Reviews 154:96–125. Crossref search: no such paper. The real 2016 Pavón-Carrasco & De Santis SAA paper is in Frontiers in Earth Science 4:40, DOI 10.3389/feart.2016.00040.

These are two fabricated citations in a single paragraph. Both citations were already present (as "unchecked") in the c3 review's stronger_arguments list. The analyst lifted them verbatim into EXP-225 without independent verification, and the decider did not citation-check during integration. This is a pipeline-level integrity concern in addition to a content concern — it suggests that when the curmudgeon flags a citation as "unchecked," downstream agents treat "unchecked" as equivalent to "verified."

The advocate_mode score flips from a 2/5 post-integration baseline to 5/5 because of the fabricated citations. The fix is trivial (two substring replacements) and must ship before any dome reader scans the page. I recommend a critical-severity issue that the decider can self-apply without analyst involvement; no EXP is required because there's no analytical work — just metadata correction.

The H(r) "field equation" label is a semantic hole the defender can open on its own (without even reading the fabricated citations) — H(r) is firmament height, not field; the dome's field-strength factor is H(r)/r, which is also radially symmetric but should be named correctly. Fixing this is a ~20-character local edit and preempts the defender's cleanest opening.

Cell centre "near 80°W" is likely too-western by ~10–15° vs. current WMM/CHAOS-7. The >100-year horizon argument is robust to this imprecision but the specific number is not defensible without a pinned source; replace with a range or qualitative phrasing.

VERDICT HOLDS: "Std Model Explains" remains correct. The axial-symmetry argument, once the citation and terminology fixes ship, is strictly stronger than the c3 baseline (exactly what the EXP intended).

Thinking about the other side of the argument (user preference): could this be a case where my citation checks missed the real paper? I ran the DOI lookup against Crossref directly and got 404 — Crossref indexes all PNAS papers, so a missing DOI there is near-conclusive. I also searched for Terra-Nova in PNAS and for SAA papers in PNAS 2017 with zero hits each. For Pavón-Carrasco & De Santis in Earth-Science Reviews: Crossref lists Earth-Science Reviews vol 154 as a real 2016 volume but contains no paper by these authors. The real papers on the same topics are findable and well-known. I could be wrong about the imprecise cell-centre number because I don't have a single pinned WMM-2025 source in front of me, so that one is flagged as "moderate" with a range recommendation rather than a hard "wrong" assertion.

PRIORITY FOR DECIDER: (1) fix both fabricated citations in a same-day surgical patch — this is the highest-urgency item in the current pipeline because the defender exposure window is open now; (2) tighten H(r) → H(r)/r or rename to "primary geometric equation"; (3) soften cell-centre "near 80°W" to a range or qualitative description; (4) pipeline structural fix: add a citation-verification step before EXP integration so that "unchecked" citations flagged by the curmudgeon get verified (or dropped) rather than passed through to production prose.

citations_verified: H(r) = 8537·exp(-r/8619) km formula — verified against raw-text/01-home.txt, 04-coordinates.txt, 07-ai-context.txt.
citations_failed: FABRICATED: Terra-Nova et al. 2017, PNAS 114:3543–3548, DOI 10.1073/pnas.1615340114. Crossref 404 on DOI; zero hits for Terra-Nova in PNAS; zero hits for SAA papers in PNAS 2017. Real paper: Terra-Nova & Amit 2017, Phys. Earth Planet. Inter. 266:39–53, DOI 10.1016/j.pepi.2017.03.002.

FABRICATED: Pávón-Carrasco & De Santis 2016, Earth-Science Reviews 154:96–125. Crossref search: not present. Real paper: Pavón-Carrasco & De Santis 2016, Frontiers in Earth Science 4:40, DOI 10.3389/feart.2016.00040.
citations_unchecked: Dome author predictions page (https://john09289.github.io/predictions) — page is live but prediction-text content was not re-pulled this cycle; assumed unchanged since c3 baseline.
Cell centre "near 80°W" claim — no single specific WMM-2025 source pinned in-paragraph; flagged as likely imprecise.
